nodes.title: Start both pointers at index 0
nodes.why: Each pointer tracks the current smallest unseen value from its array.
nodes.alternative: Copy arrays and shift from the front each step.
nodes.tradeoff: Pointer math is faster; shift-based code is shorter but slower.

nodes.title: Merge while both arrays still have values
nodes.why: As long as both sides have candidates, compare and take the smaller one.
nodes.alternative: Concatenate and sort the full result.
nodes.tradeoff: This keeps O(n + m) time instead of O((n + m) log(n + m)).

nodes.title: Push the smaller current value
nodes.why: Picking the smaller head keeps output sorted at every step.
nodes.alternative: Push one side blindly and repair order later.
nodes.tradeoff: One comparison per step, but no post-fix sorting needed.

nodes.title: Append remaining values from a
nodes.why: When b is exhausted, a already remains in sorted order.
nodes.alternative: Keep using the main loop with extra boundary checks.
nodes.tradeoff: Tiny extra loop, clearer control flow.

nodes.title: Append remaining values from b
nodes.why: Symmetric cleanup for the case where a finishes first.
nodes.alternative: Duplicate logic in condition branches above.
nodes.tradeoff: Keeps the merge logic simple and balanced.

nodes.title: Return merged output once
nodes.why: All ordering work is done incrementally, so final return is immediate.
nodes.alternative: Build output in-place in one input array.
nodes.tradeoff: Uses extra output array, but safer and easier to reason about.
